[IMAGE:0]Yes, Magic Monday's getting a late start today; after a very long four days, I fell asleep on the sofa well before midnight and had just enough presence of mind to stagger off to bed some hours later. Still, better late than never, and it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

Hello, JMG,
I was starting to get worried about you.
I hope everything is alright!

Some random questions about Christianity:

1. I received a Buddy Christ figure, a parody religious icon that first appeared in Kevin Smith’s 1999 film Dogma. Since I find traditional depictions of Christ extremely painful, I decided to use this one. Would there be a significant difference in power between traditional images and this newer version?

2. Also, would Christ be considered an egregore? Are there multiple Christ-like beings that could potentially come to Earth?

3. One last thing: U2 has a song called “40,” which is basically Psalm 40 set to music. What would be the spiritual power of more than 80,000 people singing that psalm together?

Thank you for this space, JMG!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There's always some risk in using a media-created image for religious purposes, as you might get the egregor of the media franchise rather than that of the deity! I'd encourage you to look for some other nontraditional image.

2) There is an egregor of Christ, or rather several egregors under that name. That's distinct from the being in question. As for the possibility of multiple beings of that type, why, that's a theological question about which religions disagree; you'll get one answer from most Christian theologians and a very different one from most Hindu theologians. Since I have no objective way to find out, I cheerfully admit my ignorance on the subject.

3) None whatsoever, unless they share a common intention.

replies.content:
I would be very careful utilizing any religious icon that blatantly attempts to redirect your divine devotion away from the utterly incomprehensible complexity of divinity, by channeling your devotional intent back into your own human confinement. Reaching up towards the divine is our yearning to transcend our myriad incarnate limitations, in order to catch a fleeting glimpse of what lies beyond our comprehension. We are so tiny, and gods are so vast. Any mass-produced depiction of a god that points back at you with a thumbs up and a wink was designed to inspire at least as much ego stroking as any honest divine reflection.

In fact, I would not be at all surprised if every pointing, thumbs-up, winking version of godhood were actually a stealth depiction of the Humanist god Progress, decked out in his warm-fuzzy attire, as opposed to his cold-prickly climate-doomer drag. So many of the ribald and parody portrayals of "gods" that corporations so often slip into their films and advertising are designed to minimize and marginalize the potency of godhood. Whether that's done for a good laugh or merely to inspire the kind of warm-fuzzy ego-inflation that leaves us that much more susceptible to buying their new-fangled toothpaste or gene therapy, repeatedly underestimating the potency of godhood can really mess you over. The ancient Greeks called that self-destructive habit hubris, and (unlike us) they directed a great deal of their mass-media output to warning against its brutal pitfalls.

If Christ is the divinity you have chosen for your contemplation of the divine, then go ye forth and do that thing. Contemplate Christ in all his aspects and depictions. Contemplate why you find some of those images extremely painful. Ask Christ to guide you to a better understanding of why some of his aspects and parts of his life are difficult for you to contemplate. If there is one thing that Christ in particular understands and has deep compassion for, it’s our pain and suffering!

Should Christ ever appear in one of your dreams with an approving wink and thumbs up, letting you know that that too is an expression of his infinite compassion, then create your own icon, incorporating those aspects of his multi-faceted being, along with any others he reveals to you in your dream. I promise you, that icon will be full of a power and revelation and wonder, the likes of which those corporate Progress-cultists long ago forgot how to envision. As for all their empty corporate tchotchkes, render unto the corporation the things that are the corporation's! Let those avaricious middlemen gnash their terrible teeth and mewl their growing discontent and irrelevancy, as you allow Christ himself reveal who he is to you.

— Christophe

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(Not OP)

...I'm not sure "empty corporate tchotchkes" is the emotional impression you'd be endorsing having about Buddy Christ here, or "corporate Progress-cultist" about director Kevin Smith, if you'd actually seen Dogma. For one thing, there's a layer of remove associated with the fact that, within the context of the film, Buddy Christ is part of a revival campaign by a well-meaning but unreflectively feel-good cardinal, whose improvidence sets up the plot points of the creation-destroyingly contradictory plenary indulgence and the improvised use of his blessed golf clubs as holy weapons. (Within the context of the film, "empty corporate tchotchkes" would refer more to figurines of Mooby the Golden Calf, but again, once you get to the small amount of real-world Mooby merchandise that was produced, there's a layer of critic-sympathetic satirical remove in the implied semiosis.) Buddy Christ is presented as an implied mild criticism of the cardinal's attitudes and those of the population he's playing to, in contrast to the overall presentation by the film of the divine, as something confounding that breaks easy preconceptions (even if often in a way that lends itself to an overall comedy-horror tone).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for the heads up on Dogma's subtexts. I had no memory whatsoever of Buddy Christ, so I had assumed that I hadn't ever seen the film. Your post led me to watch the trailer, only to have all those scenes and plot points come back to me. I still don't remember the Buddy Christ reference, but Mooby does ring a certain cowbell, as well as all the absurdist mapping out of angels, saints, and apostles onto the various weird tropes in Hollywood's hackneyed arsenal. Dogma does offer a biblically-literate, take-down parody of modern Christianity's many fault lines and hypocrisies, but only as trivializing fluff entertainment. It could potentially lead one to reassess one's ignored religious presumptions from a quirky new angle, but offers only fantastical canned pablum in their stead.

At this point, I tend to think of pretty much every big-name Hollywood film as mostly offering entertaining emotional hooks, designed to massage their audiences' further into their preferred ideological cul-de-sacs. If an A-list film got itself funded, how threatening to status-quo presumptions could it possibly have been? So, I'm afraid that I would consider Kevin Smith to be a rather typical corporate Progress-cultist, albeit one with an oddball John-Watersesque patina, covering over all his wealth, fame, and influence.

Within Dogma's storyline the Buddy Christ may well have been presented as an amusing piece of redemptive flotsam, somehow floating up above the various formulaic critiqued hypocrisies that film was highlighting and flogging. In my response though, I was assessing that totem's potential usefulness as an icon here in the real world, not within the alternate reality the film had posited. However it was meant to function within the film's context, I hold to my assessment that in real life Buddy Christs function as empty corporate tchotchkes, distracting prosthetics, dangled alluringly so as to keep us from ever discovering the awesome powers they are meant to keep forever occluded. The real sources of freedom and power are not being dangled on corporate lures.

— Christophe

comments.content:
Hello and good morning.

Gardening Timing:

1. When is a fortunate time to transplant baby trees (mainly oak) that have grown naturally, but are in danger of being killed by human intervention?

Gardening and Geomancy:

2. If A) it’s not fortunate to wait for that time, and B) I want to do a geomancy reading to determine the local land’s preferences (in other words, to transplant as soon as possible, or to go tell the trees that an invasive situation is imminent), would I need to be looking for modes of perfection between the 1st and 4th?

Resting Between Actions

3. I’m in circumstances where coming to a complete stop results in interference that deters the rest that is needed to bring work to fruition. I understand why, and I understand the solution, which is to rest long enough to have a stable basis to solve one issue, so that the other isn’t impacted.

I’ve tried to shift to a pattern of more quickly “moving out of range”. This sounds like a Mercury strategy to me, but as the challenge has been getting the necessary rest to build a foundation of physical strength in order to resolve the circumstances, there may be a better approach. Do you have any advice on that?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Gardening timing, gardening and geomancy, and resting between actions
replies.content:
1) You want a waning moon in an earth or water sign -- waning, because the young tree needs to put down roots and go semidormant for a little while to avoid transplant shock.

2) If you can't get them in the ground immediately, put them in pots and water them well, then plant them when the moon is in the right place. Yes, you can use geomancy with those houses, but planting them under the right moon sign is better.

3) This is so vaguely phrased that I have no idea what you're asking.

comments.title: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I'm glad to hear that you merely fell asleep. I was starting to wonder if something had happened to you.

My apologies if this one is too specific for Magic Monday.

For a while now, I've had a… roommate, I suppose. Of the non-human sort.

Going through old notes and messages, I can backdate its first appearance to some time before the 15th of last December, the day I first mentioned my nighttime hauntings to a friend. I don't think they've been going on for long before that but I'm not sure.

The visits started out harmless enough, though quite creepy. Some time after I go to bed and before I fall asleep, so most likely between midnight and 1 AM (though I don't always go to sleep at that time), something starts walking over my back or side, whichever is currently facing upwards. At first, I thought I was just feeling my heartbeat, but it didn't quite match it. The steps always lag slightly behind whenever my pulse accelerates, which tends to happen in these situations. If you imagine a housecat walking on (more or less) the spot before lying down, that's roughly what it feels like, both the movement and the size of whatever it is.

After an indeterminate amount of time of being frozen in terror, I usually manage to roll over and turn on the light, which interrupts the situation. It tends to come back when I turn off the light again, so I started performing a small banishing (not the SoP). At first, this worked great but over time, it felt as though something interfered with me expanding the protective sphere and the ritual stopped working. Granted, that was before I knew that vibrating was a term of art so I did it incorrectly, which might also be an explanation. At any rate, I switched to simply saying the Lord's Prayer, which hasn't failed me so far.

At first, this happened rarely, maybe once a week or fortnight, but then the frequency started increasing. A friend recommended I burn white sage to cleanse my room. I bought the sage but, as I was curious what was going on, decided to give the ghost cat a chance. The next time it visited me, I asked (out loud, after I found the courage to do so) what it wanted and threatened to drive it out with the sage if it wouldn't leave me alone. I got no answer except for the vague sense that I'd misunderstood its motivations and that it wasn't malicious. This gave me a couple of months of quiet, since late February.

Last month or so, it came back once again. In the same night, I dreamt that someone or something had rushed into my room and I needed to stay hidden or turn on the light. I started crawling towards the lightswitch but couldn't move (which is how I know it was a dream). I think the ensuing panic woke me but I'm not entirely sure. This was, I believe, the last of quite a few rather vivid dreams that night, each one more unpleasant than the last, though only this very last one properly qualified as a nightmare. I've had such a dream once before, probably also after a visit of my ghostly roommate, though I'm not entirely certain of that.

Then, nothing, until this morning. I was dreaming of a blackout and that I couldn't turn on the light. Or rather, every light in the house was so dim it might as well have been off. Then I woke and felt something heavy leaning against the back of my neck and head. The heavy thing purred or I imagined it did because, for a brief moment, I thought that my real cat had snuggled up to me. Then I realised that my cat wasn't in the house, nevermind the room. The weight moved (or maybe it had always been there? I felt no steps) on the top of my head. It felt as though I was about to be crushed. I tried to recite the Lord's Prayer out loud but could not move and produced no sound beyond a choked croaking. I then said it mentally and the pressure ceased. I turned on the light. Needless to say, I was quite alone in the room. For good measure, I then repeated the prayer out loud. I briefly considered going back to sleep but was too afraid to keep the light off for more than a second or two.

Needless to say, I will burn the sage today. However, I'm still curious as to what I'm dealing with here. As far as I can tell, the creature produces no sound except possibly that one purr, though of course cats tend to be quiet.

Whatever it is, I see no good reason why it should be in my bedroom. I have considered whether someone might have sent it to attack me, but while there are some people who have cause to resent me and I know at least one of them knows a few things about magic, that alone strikes me as a rather unlikely motivation. Besides, even my paranoia can't explain the timing, since I hadn't talked to any of them for about nine months when the visits started.

Do you (or anybody else) have any idea what this creature might be and what I could have done to draw it to me?

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content: I have no idea what it is -- it could be any of dozens of different classes of spirits -- but getting rid of it is a good idea. You might start by putting a small dish of vinegar in your bedroom, and refilling it regularly -- that keeps etheric substance from condensing to the point that it can affect you this way. Since you find the Lord's Prayer congenial, you might also want to pray one of the standard prayers to St. Michael the Archangel for protection against evil every night before going to bed.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content:
I'll do both of these, thanks. Does the vinegar do something different from the nail-in-salt jar you recommend in the FAQ? I imagine that it might be less smelly.
—David P.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content: Yes, the vinegar prevents etheric forms from condensing all through the room; the salt and nail discharges any etheric form that approaches it closely. I know the vinegar makes your bedroom smell like a pickle factory, but it really is the most effective way in natural magic of banishing beings that are condensing into near-physical solidity.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content:
Pickle factory it is, then. I suppose it balances out the sage smell. Thank you again!
—David P.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content: Is lemon juice not acidic enough? It might be more pleasant than vinegar.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content: That might be worth trying!

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content:
Here's what worked for me:
Practice making the sign of the cross with your eyes. Look up, look down, look side to side, while thinking "Father, Son, and Holy Spirit". From experience, once you've practiced it enough in waking life, you'll be able to do it in your sleep as well. The main plus is, it works even in sleep paralysis situations where you can't move or speak. I've never had one of the uglies stick around after, they plagued me my whole youth, and I only had to send them packing a handful of times before they gave up and stopped bothering me.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content:
I'll give that a shot, thank you!
—David P.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content: Sleeping with a Bible (ideally open to a Psalm 121) under your pillow or under your bed is traditional for ridding yourself of ghosts or hag ridings. If under the bed on the floor, it’s considered respectful to lay it on a cloth instead of the floor directly.

replies.title: Re: Roommate from hell (hopefully not literally)
replies.content:
Oddly, your new roommate doesn't sound at all malevolent to me, nor benevolent, for that matter. Have you considered whether something that's been haunting you might be ready to be resolved/shed/integrated/ or what have you? I experience all the myriad curses, blessings, bindings, etc. that accumulate within our personal ecosystems as living energy forms, with their own agendas and histories. Over time they forge complex alliances with each other and can meld into larger life forms, which psychotherapists would probably call complexes. Just as they assemble over time, they can also be dismantled with concerted effort.

Are there any potent areas of unresolved conflict in your life that you have been working on and that you might now be ready to release or allow to morph into less conflicted constellations? That purring, yet terrifying bedmate, with its rhythm just out of sync with the rhythms of your own heart, sounds like the kind of familiar that shows up in order to grab your attention and then focus it, however unwillingly, where it needs to be directed. Not exactly a friend, but definitely not an enemy either. Working with it will certainly not be a painless experience; however, not being aware of it for all those years wasn't exactly a painless experience either! Beyond any question, shifting your focus in its direction will feel scary, as many of the curses and bindings and other life forms within your larger Self will fight tooth and nail for their continued existence, attempting to maintain the status quo equilibrium within you that allows them to continue siphoning off your energy.

Have you tried journaling with automatic writing, in order to give your roommate the opportunity to introduce itself and lay out its intents? Whatever they may be, it has come to visit you in particular, so there is definitely something to be learned from its visitation. If you practice some form of divination, ask about this entity's intentions and what benefits/drawbacks will come from partnering up with it or from banishing it away. Ignoring it's call will lead to completely different outcomes, depending on whether it's intention is to harm or to heal you. If it is here to aid you in healing and you banish it away, its future manifestation may be much less ignorable (such as an incurable disease, which has been known to happen before.)

Terrifying as it feels, this may be the precise challenge you are now ready to dive into. What if this little beastie is something distinctly other than the disruptive complex of imbalancing parasites you've been enduring, but is also quite different from a guardian angel or guiding star? What if it is more like the Cheshire Cat, hard to catch a glimpse of, offering only cryptic clues and riddles, elusively fading from awareness, and confounding the deranged obsessions of any reigning berserkers within you? I believe you may have found yourself in the midst of quite a magical adventure!

— Christophe

This is just a little unnerving. Those who read my novel Journey Star will know that one of the plot points is exactly this kind of orbit, which allows a space object to approach a planet (in that case, Epsilon Eridani II) without being seen by astronomers! Are you asking what effect this object will have on the heavenly goddess Gaia in her manifestation as the third planet orbiting around a manifestation of the celestial god Sol? Or are you asking what effect it will have on the monkey line currently dominating the large-predator bracket in the thin ecosystem of Gaia's skin?

As far as that second question goes, this mysterious object will benevolently give all the loyal Gaia-denying Humanist monkeys another chance to project their delusions of their own importance onto whatever happens to be flying around in outer space at the moment. It also allows them a brief respite from contemplating their own incompetency, so they can once again imagine their monkey-shaped god Progress giving two hoots about their bleak fate. Most importantly, it allows them to rehang their fallen monkey-shaped god back up in the firmament, with the supporting wires only barely visible (at least to their true believers.) Lastly, it allows them to shriek ecstatically about their hallucination of the universe caring one iota about their over-acclaimed accomplishments, which every alien species surely must covet up to the high heavens.

How tragically predictable is this embarrassing phase of fantasy projection at this particular stage in the rapid demise of yet another monkey-shaped religion? And what self-respecting alien race would ever want to get anywhere near a bunch of monkeys this conceited and deluded?

— Christophe

comments.title: Magic Monday
comments.content:
At this page is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts (printable version here, current only to 7/14). Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests, selected from the fuller list.


May MindWinds' father Clem be healed of his spinal, blood and cardio infections and returned to good health and wholeness; and may he and his family keep up a robust sense of humor and joy in each others' continued company.

May Marko's newborn son Noah, who has been in the hospital for a cold, and Noah's mother Viktoria, who is recovering from her c-section, both be blessed with good health, strength, endurance, and protection, and may they swiftly they make a full recovery.

May Brother Kornhoer's son Travis's fistula heal, may his body have the strength to fight off infections, may his kidneys strengthen, and may his empty nose syndrome abate, so that he may have a full and healthy life ahead of him.

May Princess Cutekitten, who is sick of being sick, be healed of her ailments.

May Jack H.'s father John continue to heal from his ailments, including alcohol dependency and breathing difficulties, as much as Providence allows, to be able to enjoy more time together with his loved ones.

May Audrey's friend's daughter Katie, who died in a tragic accident June 2nd, orphaning her two children, be blessed and aided in her soul's onward journey; and may her family be comforted.

May Kevin’s sister Cynthia be cured of the hallucinations and delusions that have afflicted her, and freed from emotional distress. May she be safely healed of the physical condition that has provoked her emotions; and may she be healed of the spiritual condition that brings her to be so unsettled by it. May she come to feel calm and secure in her physical body, regardless of its level of health.

May Pierre and Julie conceive a healthy baby together. May the conception, pregnancy, birth, and recovery all be healthy and smooth for baby and for Julie.

May SLClaire's honorary daughter Beth, who is undergoing dialysis for kidney disease, be blessed, and may her kidneys be restored to full functioning.

May 1Wanderer's partner Cathy, who has bravely fought against cancer to the stage of remission, now be relieved of the unpleasant and painful side-effects from the follow-up hormonal treatment, together with the stress that this imposes on both parties; may she quickly be able to resume a normal life, and the cancer not return.

May Kallianeira's partner Patrick, who passed away on May 7th, be blessed and aided in his soul's onward journey. And may Kallianeira be soothed and strengthened to successfully cope in the face of this sudden loss.

May Linda from the Quest Bookshop of the Theosophical Society, who has developed a turbo cancer, be blessed and have a speedy and full recovery from cancer.

May Corey Benton, whose throat tumor has grown around an artery and won't be treated surgically, and who is now able to be at home from the hospital, be healed of throat cancer.
(Healing work is also welcome. Note: Healing Hands should be fine, but if offering energy work which could potentially conflict with another, please first leave a note in comments or write to randomactsofkarmasc to double check that it's safe)

May David Spangler (the esoteric teacher), who has been responding well to chemotherapy for his bladder cancer, be blessed, healed, and filled with positive energy such that he makes a full recovery.

May Giulia (Julia) in the Eastern suburbs of Cleveland Ohio be quickly healed of recurring seizures and paralysis of her left side and other neurological problems associated with a cyst on the right side of her brain and with surgery and drugs to treat it, if providence would have it, and if not, may her soul move on from this world and find peace with a minimum of further suffering for her and her family and friends.

May Liz and her baby be blessed and healthy during pregnancy, and may her husband Jay (sdi) have the grace and good humor to support his family even through times of stress and ill health.

May Debra Roberts, who has just been diagnosed with Stage 4 lung cancer, be blessed and healed to the extent that providence allows. Healing work is also welcome.

May Jack H's father John, whose aortic dissection is considered inoperable and likely fatal by his current doctors, be healed, and make a physical recovery to the full extent that providence allows, and be able to enjoy more time together with his loved ones.

May Frank R. Hartman, who lost his house in the Altadena fire, and all who have been affected by the larger conflagration be blessed and healed.

May Open Space's friend's mother Judith be blessed and healed for a complete recovery from cancer.



* * *

Guidelines for how long prayer requests stay on the list, how to word requests, how to be added to the weekly email list, how to improve the chances of your prayer being answered, and several other common questions and issues, are to be found at the Ecosophia Prayer List FAQ.

If there are any among you who might wish to join me in a bit of astrological timing, I pray each week for the health of all those with health problems on the list on the astrological hour of the Sun on Sundays, bearing in mind the Sun's rulerships of heart, brain, and vital energies. If this appeals to you, I invite you to join me.

comments.title: Characterizations of Prometheus and Rhea?
comments.content:
Good Morning JMG,

I'd like to share, for those who do video, a lecture by Michael Ward on how he believes that CS Lewis used the classical planetary personalities and energies successively and deliberately in each of his Narnia books, using Aslan to represent their energies each time. I don't think this information will be that surprising for this commentariat, but I found it worthwhile to meditate on the planetary natures as they appear in that narrative form.

The lecture: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QDpD-oTrlGE

Ward's list:
The Lion, the Witch, and the Wardrobe - Jupiter
The Horse and His Boy - Mercury
Prince Caspian - Mars
Dawn Treader - Sun
Silver Chair - Moon
Magician's Nephew - Venus
Last Battle - Saturn


Along these lines, my question is on the Promethean (Uranian) and Rhean (Neptunian) characterizations. Are there any real, or better fictional, characters you can think of that exemplify either current?

For Prometheus we have the titan himself and Johnny Appleseed, but are there any others you can think of?
For Rhea, I'm drawing a blank. Every mystic I can think of fits better in some other archetype, and the titan herself doesn't take center stage in any myths I know.

My goal would be to deliberately reference and evoke those archetypes in art and narrative.

Thanks for your help and for hosting this space.

replies.title: Re: Characterizations of Prometheus and Rhea?
replies.content: Ward's book Planet Narnia is also well worth reading. As for planetary characteristics, for Prometheus, every mad scientist in literature and legend, every inventor and pioneer, and every interesting madman is an image; think Victor von Frankenstein, Thomas Edison, Tom O' Bedlam, Emperor Norton I, and the list goes on. Rhea? You're missing the obvious. She's the big-bellied, big-breasted Cosmic Momma in all her infinite variations. She's the Egyptian goddess Nut, the Babylonian monster-goddess Tiamat, and all the other feminine images of infinite space.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Characterizations of Prometheus and Rhea?
replies.content:
Hitting the themes of communism and coercively enforced sharing of loss of conscious individual focus on tangible material outcomes, there's Comrade Sonia from We the Living.

In The Diamond Age, there are the Drummers, who live underwater in a constant collective drugged haze, and effectively practice human sacrifice through focusing their collective distributed cognition through a focal victim with no concern for the lethal physical consequences of the resulting waste heat (somehow despite the fact that the cognition itself is about a complicated physical-chemistry precision engineering problem).

The biosphere of the planet Solaris, from the eponymous novel, is a living silvery oceanic mass that resulted when its first life to evolve a primitive form of purpose overcame and subsumed all the rest without ever encountering contrary purpose. It periodically produces and breaks down hallucinatory cathedral-like fractal structures on its own, and eventually produces strange projected-force simulacra of women from the lives of the scientists studying it, as if trying to make contact across a vast gap of perspectives.

In Startide Rising, the personae of the Whale Dream with which the uplifted dolphin Captain Creideiki is confronted might qualify. They help him work through the discovery of workarounds for his brain damage, but insistently drag him out of his usual more-lucid state of consciousness on an unpredictable schedule.

replies.title: Re: Characterizations of Prometheus and Rhea?
replies.content:
That list is correct, and is something I've posted about before. Yes, each planet is a dominant thematic influence on each book, so much so that it can't be by accident. There's also the fact that CS Lewis was a medievalist, which is consistent with this, along with other influences in the series, eg the Irish seafaring folklore that informed The Voyage of the Dawn Treader.

Since Uranus and Neptune were unknown in medieval times afiak, naturally they don't figure prominently in the Narniad. Perhaps the more distant planets were known in Atlantean times (it wouldn't surprise me), but naturally we only remembered the ones that are visible via the naked eye, and had to rediscover the rest.

comments.title: SOP related questions
comments.content:
JMG,

As always, thank you for these Magic Mondays.

Last week I stumbled across J. Edward Cornelius' Aleister Crowley and the Ouija Board . I have no interest in testing or taking up the Ouija board but I'm finding this book gives insight into the background and even "technical" workings of magic, to include the Way of the Golden Section's Sphere of Protection (SOP).

Please excuse the multiple items but hope you can give confirmation I'm on the right track or point me in the right direction with the questions / statements below:

1. The author states that John Dee assignrd colors to the cardinal directions (i.e. the four watch towers and its associated "cloath" thrown on the ground). The colors were different than those assigned to the cardinal directions today. It seems the Golden Dawn wanted to "reflect the more traditional astral or "western" elemental Flashing colors (certain colors placed side by side "flash" and attracts elementals). By the wording it seems the Golden Dawn resurrected a color system older than John Dee's Enochian colors.

2. Unlike a Ouija board in which any entity may present itself, when calling the elements during the SOP we are being very specific on the type we are calling (e.g. "By the yellow gate...") which drastically decreases risk. Also the symbols we draw during the SOP bind the elementals, similar to a magician's circle and when reverse the symbols we release the elementals back through the gate.

3. During each calling the act of intense imagination is what calls in the elementals? Lighting up the astral plane with our thoughts?

4. Cornelius warns against electric lights & devices being used during the Ouija ritual and should try to reduce the amount of electrical currents in the space being worked (i.e. pull out cords from sockets). By extension, I guess this applies to most magical practice. Do you think that electrical currents would affect the SOP and Way of the Golden Section temple openings and closings?

replies.title: Re: SOP related questions
replies.content:
1) There are easily twenty different ways to assign colors to directions. These are two of them.

2) Yes, exactly.

3) It's not just one thing that invokes the elementals -- the combination of all the components is necessary.

4) I've never found that to be necessary at all. Don't assume that the conditions needed for one kind of working also apply to others!

replies.title: Re: SOP related questions
replies.content: So elementals are or start off as very mechanical/inflexible in their thought processes and their response capabilities?

replies.title: Re: SOP related questions
replies.content: Yes, very much so. One of the things they gain from mutually respectful interactions with humans is an astral form complex enough to permit creativity and flexible responses.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Typically, Martinism is Theurgy (The Work of the Gods), so Martinist ritual is, in a Christian context, calling on the Divine for protection. A Martinist mentor of mine once said "can all of this ritual be necessary when a simple prayer will suffice?" and he would have known. So absolutely these techniques will work if you are operating within the framework of the tradition, meaning you're a Christian (or at least Judeo-Christian). Otherwise, maybe not so effective, or not effective at all.

In any case, and for whichever system of religion being discussed, people greatly underestimate just how powerful an effect piety has. Offered a choice between a little breviary or the Eye of Agamotto, they'll take the eye 95 times out of 100.

comments.content:
My sympathies for being tired after a long ride... we came home from Brittany at 3 am this morning and yes, the sofa is tempting...

Speaking of Brittany, I've had one fascinating experience I'd like to share and also a related question to ask: One day we visited a tiny Island which has one of these small, sturdy granite chapels you find everywhere in Brittany (for those interested - a bit like this: https://www.gettyimages.de/detail/foto/chapel-saint-michel-des-monts-darr%C3%A9e-north-brittany-lizenzfreies-bild/1478787166 ). The chapel is dedicated to the Virgin Mary and while it is all grey rocks from the outside, it has a simple but wonderful interior with a statue of the Virgin Mary and lovely windows producing a dim, but golden light. There were a handful of tourists like us around and when we entered, a few elderly ladies started to sing what was obviously a hymn to the Virgin Mary (I'm not a Christian and I speak very little French so I can't tell more details). I have to say this was probably the most stunning experience I have ever had as guest in a church. Even after leaving the building I felt like being in some kind of golden bubble for some time.

Later in the evening I thought about the experience and for a brief time, I entered this "golden bubble" again - but this time I got also a fiery, angry sensation. At first I was confused, but then I thought I had probably entered a space where I was tolerated as guest for a short time but where I (praying to Heathen deities) am not supposed to stay? Anyway, I said my thanks and apologies and that seemed to settle the matter. But as wonderful as the first part of the experience was, as unnerving was the ferocity of the second. Thinking about it, I had another experience years ago which also involved me being delighted by music dedicated to the Virgin Mary over the course of a few weeks time and (at that time not knowing what was happening) getting a mighty "kick in the butt" after some time. It began wonderful and ended truly dreadful.

I wonder - does my reasoning above make sense? Is this (especially the not-so-nice part) something you would expect or (a thought which just entered my mind) might it point to some issues with Christianity or possibly the Virgin Mary specifically in a past life? Have you any advice in this regard?

Thank you and a good recovery from your trip :-)

Nachtgurke

comments.content:
Hello, JMG,

Last Magical Monday a question was posted about Eugene Fersen’s Science of Being and you had replied by suggesting someone should give it a try it and report back. I had a copy of his work saved on my hard drive, and felt drawn to it, for some time, so I thought, “why not?” I decided to go with the 1927 correspondence course version, so I don’t have much to report yet, although I found the meditation posture Fersen teaches (a variant of Hieratic meditation posture with the left palm facing up and the right palm facing down, if someone is interested) oddly conducive to relaxation, but this could be the novelty factor.

I also decided to retain the daily practice of SoP, meditation and divination, and am thinking about modifying SoP for the time I’m going to be working through the course.

One way to do this would be to invoke the four aspects of Universal Life Energy (Life, Mind, Truth and Love) along with the four Elements.. The least disruptive way would be to reverse his order and invoke Mind with Air, Life with with Fire, Love with Water and Truth with Earth, although I’ve encountered another attribution in Golden Dawn-related context that equated Life with Air, Light (Mind) with Fire, Love with Water and Truth (Law) with Earth.

Another way would be to use another system of seven elements and corresponding color scale from the 1923 book version of the Science of Being: Involutionary Fire (yellow-orange), Involutionary Water (red-purple), Involutionary Earth (violet-black), Evolutionary Earth (blue-violet), Evolutionary Water (blue-green), Evolutionary Fire (green-yellow) and Air or Spirit (white). So another way would be to put two aspects of Fire on the vertical axis, two aspects of Earth on North-South axis, two aspects of Water on East-West axis and Air into the center. However, this would be a radical change to the SoP.

I asked the Tarot whether I should keep my SoP unchanged, go with the former variant or with the latter, and the answer I received seems to favor the latter variant (Knight of Swords vs. King of Wands vs. The Sun). What would be your advice?

Thank you for hosting Magic Monday and sharing your wisdom and knowledge!

I read a book about creativity when, much to my surprise, I came across a mention of Scott Adams (of Dilbert fame) — that Scott Adams who is sometimes mentioned on the other blog for his attitude towards the unvaccinated, and because he got prostrate cancer. I was even more surprised when I learned via that book that apparently, Adams had used affirmations with great success back before he became a cartoonist. According to the book, the whole thing started as a lark when he was taking a hypnosis class. He used an affirmation and got a date with a girl that was out of his league (according to him), then went on to affirm becoming successful at investing in the stock market, and finally, after affirming to pass a test that would get him into his dream school, he affirmed that he’d become a famous cartoonist. Every time, he got exactly what he wanted.

Now, I’m not one to ask facetiously why he isn’t affirming that he’ll overcome his illness, and if he’s doing so, why he hasn’t healed yet. But the whole thing immediately reminded me of a similar case. Some years back, a book was making waves in the German New Age scene, called Bestellungen beim Universum (which can be translated as ‘placing orders with the universe’). The author, Bärbel Mohr, claimed to have gotten the man of her dreams, an honest-to-god castle to live in, and various other stuff by basically treating the universe like a mail-order warehouse. Then she got cancer and died, leaving two small children behind.

I’m not saying that if you do affirmations to get material success (and stuff) you’ll get cancer — that sounds like masturbation will make you blind. But I do wonder a) if there’s some kind of mechanism one should be aware of to avoid, err, getting that kind of invoice from the universal mail-order warehouse, and b) why affirmations seemed to fail for these people when it came to overcoming their illness, because I’m pretty sure they did try and use them; they had used affirmations with spectacular success before, after all.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Thanks for carving out a day for us, as always. May you be blessed !

My question is about the “yes/no” of geomancy and reading into the characters too much.

I like to do the quick and dirty “cast one character” sometimes. Below is an example of how my brain translates it.

“Should I take a walk today?” - answer: Tristitia (No)
My brain “A walk would be grounding”

“Should I do an Air working tonight?” - answer: Rubeus (no)
My brain: “This is a clear sign that air is active”

You get the idea.


My question is: The geomantic figures are defined into positive and negatives, but how much wiggle room should we give? The symbols really come with much depth, yet I know Geomancy is one of the straight forward methods available. Surely there is more being communicated than just yes or no?

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: This is why I say that the most effective way to fail at geomancy is to overthink the answers you get. If you ask a yes or no question, the positive figures are yes, the negative figures are no, full stop, end of sentence. If you go for wiggle room instead, doubtless you'll get a deeper and more sophisticated answer, but odds are it will be wrong. The answer you get by paying attention to positive and negative alone may be simplistic but it'll be correct.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:

My follow up question would be for the daily cast. That’s how I’ve been practicing…

With the daily cast, I tend to read into the symbols a bit more. If the question is not really a yes/no answer - like a “what do I have to know about today?” I do tend to read into it more.

Would reading into them be a valid option if the question is open ended and not a yes/no?

Thank you again

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: If it's open-ended, yes, go for it -- but always, once the day's over, go back to it and compare your interpretation to what happened. That'll help you calibrate your interpretations and learn to avoid reading into a chart what you wanted to see there.

replies.content: Economists' predictions are, after all, both deep and sophisticated.

comments.title: Star magic
comments.content:
I've been gradually getting over my rationalist conditioning, and realizing that all sorts of phenomena have spirits associated with them. Which leads me to ask, what sort of spirits are in the stars? Are they friendly to us, indifferent, or otherwise? I assume you can work magic with them, but star magic is probably an advanced technique, and I'm not sure if that's a good idea?

And speaking of which, where did that whole thing about "wishing on a star" originate? Surely the roots run deeper than Pinocchio, which seems to be built on older myths...

replies.title: Re: Star magic
replies.content:
According to some occult teachings, there are twelve star logoi whose responsibility is to mediate the influences of the rest of the universe to our solar system. Associated with each of them is a community of star-spirits who do this with individual stars. You can work magic with them, but it's like other astrological magic -- all you can do is find a moment when the influences are strong anyway, and do things with that descending current of force. You can't get the spirits to do anything for you other than that.

"Wishing on a star" is a dim folk memory of astrological magic, and of course Disney and that singing insect with a covertly theological name -- "Jiminy Cricket" was a euphemism for "Jesus Christ," the sort of thing you could say around your devout great-aunt when you wanted to swear -- made it stupid. After I first watched the movie version of Pinocchio in childhood, and heard this...

"When you wish upon a star,
Makes no difference who you are,
Anything that you desire will come to you..."

...I tried it, of course, and got nothing. That was one of the early experiences -- the whole Santa Claus fraud was another -- that convinced me that adults could not be trusted to tell me the truth. I used to wonder sometimes, when I was lying in bed failing to get to sleep, if they were sitting out there in the living room laughing at children who were stupid enough to believe them.

replies.title: Re: Star magic
replies.content:
Most informative, thanks. I guess it's like working with the light of the full moon, in that you can only channel what comes to you on its own timetable.

And I figured as much. I do think the original Pinocchio has many good qualities (far better than current-year bilge), but you make a good point -- telling sweet, honeyed lies to children ultimately does more harm than good.

And it's not limited to kids either, sometimes entire societies lie to themselves this way. An example of this is Flight 93 on 9/11, and the inspiring, intrepid tale of a passenger revolt that never happened, because it was shot down by the US Air Force. I won't get into my reasoning, since this is a tangent veering too far off, but it's common sense when you think about it. I only bring it up since it's in that same class of lovely, inspiring lies, which when you figure them out bring nothing but disillusionment.

replies.title: Re: Star magic
replies.content:
Funny you should speak of "one of the early experiences [...] that convinced me that adults could not be trusted to tell me the truth", in response to a question about derationalismization. Eliezer Yudkowsky wrote in "No Safe Defense, Not Even Science" ( https://lesswrong.com/lw/qf/no-safe-defense-not-even-science ):

> Of the people I know who are reaching upward as rationalists, who volunteer information about their childhoods, there is a surprising tendency to hear things like: "My family joined a cult and I had to break out," or "One of my parents was clinically insane and I had to learn to filter out reality from their madness."
>
> My own experience with growing up in an Orthodox Jewish family seems tame by comparison... but it accomplished the same outcome: It broke my core emotional trust in the sanity of the people around me.
>
> Until this core emotional trust is broken, you don't start growing as a rationalist. I have trouble putting into words why this is so. Maybe any unusual skills you acquire—anything that makes you unusually rational—requires you to zig when other people zag. Maybe that's just too scary, if the world still seems like a sane place unto you.
>
> Or maybe you don't bother putting in the hard work to be extra bonus sane, if normality doesn't scare the hell out of you.

So, like, he had a similar experience, but became convinced of a broader proposition: that other people's actual sincere and carefully-considered beliefs could not be trusted to tell him the truth. Further on in the essay, he extends this to the proposition that even one's sincere following of the rules and norms of Science can't be trusted to tell one the truth (such as could have been immediately found by some other procedure lacking Science's obvious flaws), and likewise for every other concrete procedure, and also that in some sense one's brain cannot be trusted to tell one the truth:

> So don't think that if you only follow the rules of Science, that makes your reasoning defensible.
>
> There is no known procedure you can follow that makes your reasoning defensible.
>
> There is no known set of injunctions which you can satisfy, and know that you will not have been a fool.
>
> There is no known morality-of-reasoning that you can do your best to obey, and know that you are thereby shielded from criticism.
>
> No, not even if you turn to Bayescraft. It's much harder to use and you'll never be sure that you're doing it right.
>
> The discipline of Bayescraft is younger by far than the discipline of Science. You will find no textbooks, no elderly mentors, no histories written of success and failure, no hard-and-fast rules laid down. You will have to study cognitive biases, and probability theory, and evolutionary psychology, and social psychology, and other cognitive sciences, and Artificial Intelligence—and think through for yourself how to apply all this knowledge to the case of correcting yourself, since that isn't yet in the textbooks.
>
> You don't know what your own mind is really doing. They find a new cognitive bias every week and you're never sure if you've corrected for it, or overcorrected.
>
> The formal math is impossible to apply. It doesn't break down as easily as John Q. Unbeliever thinks, but you're never really sure where the foundations come from. [...]

I don't know if you've picked up on this yet, but one thing that tends to go along with rationality and rationalism is a propensity—and, so far as I can distinguish, that propensity may well be sincere—to take human belief states that might plausibly have been caused by a known pattern of errors, and to then interpret those belief states as having been most likely caused by that known pattern of errors. Typically, this is done out of a profound fear of becoming irrecoverably trapped in some surprise form of individual or collective self-delusion, that would engage in a sort of "regulatory capture" corruption of the standards of judgement about beliefs and evidence that might have otherwise saved the person from epistemic perdition. (In connection with this idea of "pattern of errors"—the main cultural schism within broader rationality culture seems to be between people who define "pattern of errors" according to engineering-like conceptual principles, and those who define "pattern of errors" according to social categorization intuitions such as contrasts between the social intuitions of "psychologically normal credible scientist" vs. "runaway crackpot" or "exploitativeness-complicit cultist".)

This is related to a point I keep harping on, about the potentially exonerated moral standing of rationalists and other people in positions of public trust, who interpret the invariable unwillingness of patterns of supernatural deviation from chance expectation, to decisively and consistently defy alternative materialist explanations beyond a certain point of ambiguity, as evidence that the supernatural is a delusion. But to really argue for that requires opening up this whole cosmological can of worms, and I feel like if I don't present the rest of the argument all in one place perfectly and well under the 16,000 character dreamwidth comment limit, then you'll just instead get inoculated against the conclusion somehow.

(Incidentally, one of the "one of my parents was clinically insane and I had to learn to filter out reality from their madness" people he's referring to was the probable patient zero of the Luciferian-influence outbreak we had that, after mutating a bit, got half a dozen people killed. I suspect that they caught it from whoever the person was who was running lucifer.com back ca. 2005, that the then-flagship organization of what would later also become the rationality movement accepted web hosting from.

There's a connnection, here, to the cosmological can of worms mentioned above. It seems hardly coincidental that my degree of belief in this conjecture about an outbreak of Luciferian influence fits in with a certain infamously-known pattern of error and delusion: the pattern exhibited by people whose social self-image self-restraint of their schizoness is failing visibly enough to make them potentially too volatile to maintain ties with. I've once or twice had to push back on people repeating my hypothesis back to me as the more schizo-sounding 'X is the devil', to correct it to 'X has a Luciferian devotion', and with most other people I couldn't even have done that without it having just come off as me pathetically trying to pretend to myself that I couldn't see the obvious schizoness of the belief. ...I can't even say, "look, it's not schizo by the diagnostic criteria, because it's subculturally normal", because there is no relevant rationalist-occultist subculture. My beliefs are just what would be subculturally normal, if there were. I (unreflectively-)wish that there actually was one, so that I could say something like that.)

replies.content: In my experience, adults cannot even be trusted to tell themselves the truth.

replies.title: Re: Atheism
replies.content: This is not too surprising, since Atheism is ultimately the latest branch of Protestantism, continuing its trend of rebelling against authority and focusing on the individual. In the past, they argued for reading the Scripture yourself instead of relying on Church fathers, priests, ecclesiastical hierarchy and so on. Now, the atheists argue against relying on the authority of ancient texts, and tell you to be your own god and worship your own intellect. One reason why that fedora reddit post about being "enlightened by your own intelligence" was so widely mocked is because there was a seed of truth in it. And that's not all -- atheists inherit various other qualities from their Protestant forebears, such as evangelical zeal and insistence on correct belief. We can also see this on the Left in general, because it's all related. The reverberations of the Protestant Reformation are still with us 500 years later.

replies.title: Re: Atheism

Like Augustus Frost not being allowed to suspect that his p-zombie ideology is incoherent nonsense until the moment of his death in That Hideous Strength?

Patrick

replies.title: Re: Atheism
replies.content: Exactly. That scene has come to my mind tolerably often when dealing with such people.

replies.title: Re: Atheism
replies.content:
This... is more or less what happened with the Luciferian-outbreak patient X that I was making claims about in the other thread. Except they were a materialist who considered it all to be metaphors and psychological attractor-structures, for describing and manipulating Gurdjieffian collective and individual meaningless blinders and limiters, that were making everyone dysfunctional for not being aware of and transcending them. But they kept playing headgames with themselves and others, probably charged in their mind with those symbols, right up to and through their psychotic break, at which point I presume the distinction between "just metaphors and attractor structures" and "independently real" stopped really mattering subjectively. And afterwards, they were in this weird headspace the way Nick Land was, where the formal materialism didn't seem like the sort of thing that could really get in the way of the malefic effects anymore.

A weird point is that, the one time (well before the break) that I tried to defend to them my position about the supernatural, we were sitting under this minimall awning after hours. Right when I was about to press a technical point about how prior beliefs could inform interpretation in such a way as to create a correlated posterior belief where each mixture component of the posterior belief distribution might naively look like circular reasoning to the other (not in so many words), this pigeon flew up in a noisy attention-getting way to a nest in the awning behind them, and in my field of view above them, started periodically whipping their head back and forth, like a Western-culture human shaking their head "no" but faster (adapted to pigeon timescales?), and more dissociated like one in a trance. So I stopped.

I'm not really sure how trying to push the argument further at that point would have made things worse. Is it that it would have been worse for me, or that their believing in the actual supernatural would somehow make them contribute to even worse consequences for everyone else?

replies.title: Re: Atheism
replies.content:
For what it's worth, as a further data point, my own experience was rather less dire than the pattern our host outlines, and ended up more as "drop the atheism." When I first gave magic and prayer a try, I figured I was just interacting with the patterns in my deep mind via symbolism and the like. I found the experience rather underwhelming, didn't see any result that I could identify, and couldn't shake the feeling that I was just playing pretend/talking to myself, so I dropped the practices. A while later, I read A World Full of Gods and had found enough of value in our host's other writings to take it seriously, so I said to myself "well, it's worth a shot taking the Gods seriously as 'really real,'" and then I took up daily meditation, ritual, and divination (I hadn't taken meditation as seriously when I tried the first time). Whelp, within a few weeks, I had a most striking religious experience and decided "maybe there's something to all this religion stuff after all!" and haven't looked back since.

I count myself lucky that a) I was never attracted to the "gimme gimme!" kind of occultism, and so didn't give that a try even when I didn't have spiritual guardrails to haul me back from selfish stupidity, and b) since my atheistic frame was "I'm trying to interact with the patterns in my own mind," I saw demons as bad patterns to be avoided, and so didn't stumble into actually interacting with nasty things I would have thought were imaginary.

None of which is to say I recommend atheistic occultism as a cure for atheism! I just lucked out that it was a stepping stone for me on a path to a better way.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Atheism
replies.content: Thank you for this! I'm glad to hear that it can work out more constructively.
